**Cheick Kongo** (born May 17, 1975) is a French [mixed martial artist](/wiki/Mixed_martial_artist "Mixed martial artist") and former [kickboxer](/wiki/Kickboxer "Kickboxer") who fights in the [Heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)") division. A professional MMA competitor since 2001, he has also competed for the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") and [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cheick_Kongo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born to a [Burkinabé](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso") father and a [Congolese](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") mother, Kongo began training in martial arts when he was 5 years old, learning [Kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo") and [Karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"). As he grew older, he had already received a black belt in both styles and began to expand his repertoire and trained in [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai"), [Savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate"), and [Greco Roman Wrestling](/wiki/Greco_Roman_Wrestling "Greco Roman Wrestling") at the age of 19. At the age of 23 he learned [Pencak Silat](/wiki/Pencak_Silat "Pencak Silat") Persaudaraan Setia Hati Terate under the tutelage of Charles Joussoat and Franck Ropers, who learned it from C. N. Hardjono Turpijn, the pioneer of Pencak Silat PSHT in Europe.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Kongo was introduced to [Shootfighting](/wiki/Shootfighting "Shootfighting") through [Bas Rutten](/wiki/Bas_Rutten "Bas Rutten") at age 25, and started his career in mixed martial arts in 2001. He is also a cousin of rugby star [Fulgence Ouedraogo](/wiki/Fulgence_Ouedraogo "Fulgence Ouedraogo"), the French back-rower. Cheick is also a columnist for the French MMA magazine, Top Fight.

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cheick_Kongo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

On June 10, 2001, Kongo began his professional career by knocking out Doog Ward. Kongo then followed up that win with a TKO over [Dave van der Veen](/wiki/Dave_van_der_Veen "Dave van der Veen"). He then lost his third professional MMA fight to [Rodney Faverus](/wiki/Rodney_Faverus "Rodney Faverus") by decision. He faced [Gilbert Yvel](/wiki/Gilbert_Yvel "Gilbert Yvel") on May 20, losing for the second time in his career when he tapped to strikes in the second round. He trained with Anderson Silva and Wanderlei Silva in the early days of [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships").

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cheick_Kongo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

Kongo entered the UFC to fight Gilbert Aldana at *[UFC 61](/wiki/UFC_61 "UFC 61")*, whom he beat by TKO due to a doctor stoppage. Kongo followed up with a win over UFC newcomer [Christian Wellisch](/wiki/Christian_Wellisch "Christian Wellisch") by KO due to a knee strike, before losing to UFC newcomer [Carmelo Marrero](/wiki/Carmelo_Marrero "Carmelo Marrero") at *[UFC 64](/wiki/UFC_64 "UFC 64")*. Then, Kongo defeated [Assuério Silva](/wiki/Assu%C3%A9rio_Silva "Assuério Silva") by majority decision at *[UFC 70](/wiki/UFC_70 "UFC 70")*.

At *[UFC 75](/wiki/UFC_75 "UFC 75")* on September 8, 2007, he defeated [Mirko Cro Cop](/wiki/Mirko_Cro_Cop "Mirko Cro Cop") by unanimous decision. Though there was controversy as Kongo kneed Cro Cop in the groin several times without having any points deducted, inspiring American MMA fans to give Kongo the nickname "[Cup](/wiki/Jockstrap#Protective_cup "Jockstrap") Cheick".

He fought against [Heath Herring](/wiki/Heath_Herring "Heath Herring") at *[UFC 82](/wiki/UFC_82 "UFC 82")*, losing by split decision. At *[UFC 87](/wiki/UFC_87 "UFC 87")* he fought against UFC newcomer [Dan "The Viking" Evensen](/wiki/Dan_Evensen "Dan Evensen"), whom he defeated by first-round TKO.

Kongo fought at [UFC 92](/wiki/UFC_92 "UFC 92") on December 27, 2008, where he beat former [Cage Rage](/wiki/Cage_Rage "Cage Rage") British Heavyweight champion Mostapha al-Turk by TKO. Kongo dropped Al Turk with two right hands. Then fired on a vicious ground and pound attack, Kongo pounced on al-Turk with elbows and punches, opening up a big cut above the eye.[[4]](#cite_note-sherdog.com-4) Referee Steve Mazzagatti stopped the fight after 4:37 of the first round.[[4]](#cite_note-sherdog.com-4)

In his next fight he defeated [Antoni Hardonk](/wiki/Antoni_Hardonk "Antoni Hardonk") at [UFC 97](/wiki/UFC_97 "UFC 97") by second-round TKO.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Kongo replaced Heath Herring on three weeks notice at [UFC 99](/wiki/UFC_99 "UFC 99") to face the up-and-coming [Cain Velasquez](/wiki/Cain_Velasquez "Cain Velasquez") in the co-main event. Kongo lost to Velasquez by unanimous decision in a one-sided affair. He did however, manage to drop Velasquez on two occasions with straight punches. It had been rumoured that if he was to defeat Velasquez, Kongo would earn a title shot against the winner of the [UFC 100](/wiki/UFC_100 "UFC 100") Heavyweight Championship between [Brock Lesnar](/wiki/Brock_Lesnar "Brock Lesnar") and [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir"). Mir lost his bout against Lesnar and actually ended up fighting Kongo at [UFC 107](/wiki/UFC_107 "UFC 107"). During the pre-fight talk, Mir stated that Kongo had no ground game, so at the weigh ins Cheick Kongo turned his back to Mir, to be disrespectful. Mir won the fight by a submission. After connecting with a left overhand punch that dropped Kongo, Mir finished him with a guillotine choke, rendering Kongo unconscious just 72 seconds into the first round.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Kongo defeated [Paul Buentello](/wiki/Paul_Buentello "Paul Buentello") on March 21, 2010, at [UFC Live: Vera vs. Jones](/wiki/UFC_Live:_Vera_vs._Jones "UFC Live: Vera vs. Jones") by submission due to elbows.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Kongo was expected to face [The Ultimate Fighter 10](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_10 "The Ultimate Fighter 10") winner [Roy Nelson](/wiki/Roy_Nelson_(fighter) "Roy Nelson (fighter)") on July 3, 2010, at [UFC 116](/wiki/UFC_116 "UFC 116") but a back injury to Kongo has caused the cancellation of the fight.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Kongo's next fight was against undefeated [Travis Browne](/wiki/Travis_Browne "Travis Browne") at [UFC 120](/wiki/UFC_120 "UFC 120").[[9]](#cite_note-9) They fought to a unanimous draw. Kongo repeatedly grabbed Browne's shorts and had 1 point deducted in the final round.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Kongo fought [Pat Barry](/wiki/Pat_Barry_(fighter) "Pat Barry (fighter)") on June 26, 2011, at [UFC on Versus 4](/wiki/UFC_on_Versus_4 "UFC on Versus 4").[[11]](#cite_note-11) His fight against Barry became the main event since [Nate Marquardt](/wiki/Nate_Marquardt "Nate Marquardt") was not medically cleared for his welterweight debut against [Rick Story](/wiki/Rick_Story "Rick Story").[[12]](#cite_note-12) During the bout Kongo was dropped by a looping right hand. Visibly rocked, Kongo got back to his feet but was dropped again seconds later by another hard punch. Despite being dropped twice, Kongo was able to knock Barry out with a hard overhand right to the ear, followed by an uppercut to the chin to secure a win midway through the first round. [[13]](#cite_note-13)

Kongo faced [Matt Mitrione](/wiki/Matt_Mitrione "Matt Mitrione") on October 29, 2011, at [UFC 137](/wiki/UFC_137 "UFC 137"). He won the fight via unanimous decision by utilizing his reach with his jab and securing multiple takedowns.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Kongo faced [Mark Hunt](/wiki/Mark_Hunt "Mark Hunt") on February 26, 2012, at [UFC 144](/wiki/UFC_144 "UFC 144").[[15]](#cite_note-15) He lost the fight via TKO in the first round.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Kongo was expected to face [Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rodrigo_Nogueira "Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira") on July 21, 2012, at [UFC 149](/wiki/2012_in_UFC#UFC_149:_Faber_vs._Barão "2012 in UFC").[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, Nogueira pulled out of the bout, citing that an arm injury sustained in his last bout against [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir") had not healed enough to resume the proper training and was replaced by [Shawn Jordan](/wiki/Shawn_Jordan "Shawn Jordan").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Kongo defeated Jordan via unanimous decision.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Kongo faced [Roy Nelson](/wiki/Roy_Nelson_(fighter) "Roy Nelson (fighter)") on April 27, 2013, at [UFC 159](/wiki/UFC_159 "UFC 159"), they were supposed to meet before at UFC 116.[[20]](#cite_note-20) He lost the fight via knockout in the first round. After his loss to Nelson, Kongo's UFC contract expired and he was released from the UFC.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Before UFC 159 the UFC offered Kongo a four-fight deal, which he turned down. Kongo left the UFC with a 11-6-1 record.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On May 4, 2013, Kongo stated that he is on good terms with the UFC and was quoted saying: "Don't believe he said-she said stories. I have not been cut by the UFC. I am Cheick Kongo so I know best what's next for me. It doesn't mean that I am leaving the UFC, it doesn't mean that I am staying – it means this is my life." Kongo has stated that he is open to the possibility of resigning a contract with the UFC.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Bellator MMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cheick_Kongo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bellator MMA")]

Shortly after it was announced that Cheick's teammate [Rampage Jackson](/wiki/Rampage_Jackson "Rampage Jackson") was going to [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA"), it was stated that Kongo will be following his teammate.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Kongo's move to [Bellator](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA") was official on August 28, 2013.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Kongo made his debut in the [Season 9 Heavyweight tournament](/wiki/Bellator_MMA:_Season_Nine#Heavyweight_tournament_bracket "Bellator MMA: Season Nine"). He faced Mark Godbeer on October 4, 2013, at [Bellator 102](/wiki/Bellator_MMA:_Season_Nine#Bellator_CII "Bellator MMA: Season Nine").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Kongo won the fight via TKO in the second round.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

He was then expected to face [Vinicius Queiroz](/wiki/Vinicius_Queiroz "Vinicius Queiroz") in the tournament finals, on November 2, 2013, at [Bellator 106](/wiki/Bellator_MMA:_Season_Nine#Bellator_CVI "Bellator MMA: Season Nine").[[28]](#cite_note-28) However, due to a change in the line up, the bout was canceled.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Bout between Kongo and Queiroz was then expected to take place on November 8, 2013, at [Bellator 107](/wiki/Bellator_107 "Bellator 107").[[30]](#cite_note-30) However, Queiroz was later forced to withdraw from the bout due to a knee injury[[31]](#cite_note-31) and was replaced by [Peter Graham](/wiki/Peter_Graham_(kickboxer) "Peter Graham (kickboxer)").[[32]](#cite_note-32) Kongo won the fight via unanimous decision.

Kongo faced undefeated Bellator Heavyweight Champion [Vitaly Minakov](/wiki/Vitaly_Minakov "Vitaly Minakov") at Bellator 115 on April 4, 2014.[[33]](#cite_note-33) He lost the back-and-forth fight via unanimous decision. Kongo faced Eric Smith at [Bellator 120](/wiki/Bellator_120 "Bellator 120") on May 17, 2014.[[34]](#cite_note-34) He won the fight via TKO by way of knees and punches at 4:35 of Round 2.

Kongo faced fellow striker [Lavar Johnson](/wiki/Lavar_Johnson "Lavar Johnson") at [Bellator 123](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2014#Bellator_123 "Bellator MMA in 2014") on September 5, 2014.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) He won the fight via rear-naked choke submission in the first round. This marked his first submission win that was not caused by strikes since 2003.

Kongo faced [King Mo Lawal](/wiki/Muhammed_Lawal "Muhammed Lawal") at [Bellator 134: The British Invasion](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2015#Bellator_134 "Bellator MMA in 2015") on February 27, 2015.[[37]](#cite_note-37) He lost the fight via split decision.

Kongo faced former [Bellator Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/List_of_Bellator_champions#Heavyweight_World_Championship "List of Bellator champions") [Alexander Volkov](/wiki/Alexander_Volkov_(fighter) "Alexander Volkov (fighter)") on June 26, 2015, at [Bellator 139](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2015#Bellator_139 "Bellator MMA in 2015").[[38]](#cite_note-38) He won the fight via unanimous decision.

Kongo faced [Vinicius Queiroz](/wiki/Vinicius_Queiroz "Vinicius Queiroz") at [Bellator 150](/wiki/Bellator_150 "Bellator 150") on February 26, 2016. In the third round, Kongo was dropped by a right hand, however, he was able to recover and win a split decision.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Kongo faced [Tony Johnson](/wiki/Tony_Johnson_(fighter) "Tony Johnson (fighter)") at [Bellator 161](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2016#Bellator_161 "Bellator MMA in 2016") on September 16, 2016. He won the fight via majority decision.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Kongo faced former training partner [Oli Thompson](/wiki/Oli_Thompson "Oli Thompson") at [Bellator 172](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2017#Bellator_172 "Bellator MMA in 2017") on February 18, 2017. He won the fight by unanimous decision.

Kongo faced [Augusto Sakai](/wiki/Augusto_Sakai "Augusto Sakai") at [Bellator 179](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2017#Bellator_179 "Bellator MMA in 2017") on May 19, 2017. He won the fight via split decision.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Kongo faced [Javy Ayala](/wiki/Javy_Ayala "Javy Ayala") at [Bellator 199](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2018#Bellator_199 "Bellator MMA in 2018") on May 12 in San Jose, CA.[[42]](#cite_note-42) He won the fight via knockout in the first round.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Kongo faced [Timothy Johnson](/wiki/Timothy_Johnson_(fighter) "Timothy Johnson (fighter)") on October 13, 2018, at [Bellator 208](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2018#Bellator_208 "Bellator MMA in 2018").[[44]](#cite_note-44) He won the fight by knockout in the first round.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Kongo faced [Vitaly Minakov](/wiki/Vitaly_Minakov "Vitaly Minakov") for a second time on February 16, 2019, at [Bellator 216](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2019#Bellator_2016 "Bellator MMA in 2019")[[46]](#cite_note-46) He won the fight by unanimous decision.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

On July 11, 2019, it was announced that Kongo will be facing [Ryan Bader](/wiki/Ryan_Bader "Ryan Bader") for the [Bellator Heavyweight World Championship](/wiki/List_of_Bellator_MMA_champions#Heavyweight_championship "List of Bellator MMA champions") on September 7 at [Bellator 226](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2019#Bellator_226 "Bellator MMA in 2019").[[48]](#cite_note-48) The bout ended in a no contest at 3:52 of the first round after an accidental eye poke from Bader rendered Kongo unable to continue.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

On May 4, 2020, it was revealed that Kongo had signed a multi-fight contract with Bellator.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

On October 10, 2020, Kongo fought in a rematch [Timothy Johnson](/wiki/Timothy_Johnson_(fighter) "Timothy Johnson (fighter)") at [Bellator 248](/wiki/Bellator_MMA_in_2020#Bellator_248 "Bellator MMA in 2020") in [Paris, France](/wiki/Paris,_France "Paris, France"). He lost via split decision.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Kongo faced [Sergei Kharitonov](/wiki/Sergei_Kharitonov "Sergei Kharitonov") on August 20, 2021, at [Bellator 265](/wiki/Bellator_265 "Bellator 265").[[52]](#cite_note-52) He won the fight via a rear-naked choke in round two.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Kongo faced [Ryan Bader](/wiki/Ryan_Bader "Ryan Bader") for the [Bellator Heavyweight World Championship](/wiki/Bellator_Heavyweight_World_Championship "Bellator Heavyweight World Championship") on May 6, 2022, at [Bellator 280](/wiki/Bellator_280 "Bellator 280").[[54]](#cite_note-54) He lost the bout via unanimous decision.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
